chemistry.scope_summary: Study focused on optimization of photochemical reactors for α-pinene oxygenation using photogenerated singlet oxygen. Various reactor types (immersed-well, annular recirculating, microreactors) and light sources (LEDs, fluorescent, arc lamps) were compared.

optimization_table.key_findings: 416 nm LED array showed highest conversion in microreactor, better efficiency than conventional Hg MP lamp due to spectral overlap with TPP absorption

figures.flow_path: reaction mixture flows through microreactor channels with oxygen introduction
figures.key_information: Multiple reactor configurations (a-e) showing different light sources: 125W Hg lamp, Xe arc lamp, LED arrays at 416nm and 524nm, and metal halide lamp. All setups include oxygen flow and serpentine microreactor channels with external illumination.
figures.caption: Figure 1.  A summary of photochemical reactors and lamps investigated in this study; schematic illustration of different operating regimes. (a) An immersed well (batch) reactor. (b) A recirculating annular reactor. (c) A microreactor in a continuous operation. (d) A microreactor operating with recirculation. (e) A microreactor with segmented gas − liquid flow. | Figure 2.  Oxygenation of α-pinene to pinocarvone.

Figure 1.  A summary of photochemical reactors and lamps investigated in this study; schematic illustration of different operating regimes. (a) An immersed well (batch) reactor. (b) A recirculating annular reactor. (c) A microreactor in a continuous operation. (d) A microreactor operating with recirculation. (e) A microreactor with segmented gas − liquid flow. | Figure 2.  Oxygenation of α-pinene to pinocarvone.

figures.trend: Conversion increases with time, with 420 nm Actinic showing fastest rate, followed by 524 nm LED, then Xe arc and Hg medium pressure sources
figures.caption: Figure 3.  Conversion of α-pinene in the annular recirculating photoreactor illuminated by 524 nm LED (○), 420 nm fluorescent (×), and Xe arc lamps (●) compared with that in the immersion well reactor illuminated by Hg MP lamp (△).

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Long reaction times (420 min), lower space-time yields, inefficient light utilization in conventional batch reactors
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Significantly reduced residence time (14.4 min vs 420 min), better light absorption in miniaturized channels, higher space-time yields (up to 10x improvement), precise control of gas-liquid contact
translation_logic.what_changed: Reactor geometry from immersed-well to microreactor, light source from 125W Hg MP lamp to 416nm LED array, residence time from 420 min to 14.4 min, pressure increased to 6 bar
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Temperature (20°C), solvent (CH2Cl2), concentration (0.36 M), oxygen atmosphere
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Reduced reaction volume, better heat dissipation in microchannels, precise pressure control

figure_descriptions: Multiple reactor configurations showing different light sources and operating modes
Optimization plots comparing different light sources and reactor types
Space-time yield comparisons across reactor configurations

notes: Complete study comparing batch and microreactor systems for photochemical oxygenation. Time reduction factor calculated from 420 min batch vs 14.4 min flow residence time. No BPR visible in reactor scheme but 6 bar back pressure reported in text. Economic optimization suggests 240 μm optimal channel depth.
